top_comments.text: Have you watched the watchman tv show yet? Never read the books or watched the movie but this series caught me so off guard. It was wonderful and strange. At least strange from pre 2020 standards. If squid fell from the sky tomorrow I’d just brush it off at this point.

At our org we run code-server in docker with no network access except pre-allowed DNS, and a white listed squid proxy, due to this.

It works really well for us and prevents potentially bad extensions, python libraries, etc exfilling our code, data, etc.

> My only gripe is Cloudflare has to /store/ content on its servers, so it's not really an ISP in the traditional sense.

Traditionally (at least in my experience), back in the pre-HTTPS-everywhere days, ISPs often had Squid proxies, sometimes optional (had to be manually configured) and sometimes "transparent" (the ISP's router forcefully forwarded all connections matching TCP destination port 80 to the Squid proxy). IMHO, that's a close enough analogue to

Note that:

  At the time of this writing, RHEL 6.7 is still pre-beta
  and this bug was found in an *UNRELEASED* update to squid.
Bad enough, but not like it's out in production.

Whoah, I'd almost completely forgotten Kuro5hin, and a lot of other people obviously have, too, as the site is a ghost town. It was a really neat pre-reddit user-generated social media site; kind of a more democratic Slashdot. I knew the founder in some capacity (I don't even remember how/why, now, or their name...maybe because I worked on Squid or because I worked in Perl), and used to advertise my old company on Kuro5hin to be able to send some money their way.

I quite liked i

contrarian_analysis.bull_case_flaw: Their core pitch is 'AI-powered grid planning in your browser,' but they provide no technical details, no live product, and zero public code or demos. The absence of specifics on their AI capabilities, data sources, or differentiation from existing grid planning tools makes their story sound more like a vision deck than an operational startup.
contrarian_analysis.elephant_in_room: Where is the product? Why is everything a 404, and why is there no evidence of actual AI technology, customer traction, or even a functioning prototype?

timing_assessment.reasoning: The market for AI-driven grid planning is still nascent, and utilities move slowly. Without clear regulatory buy-in, validated models, or deep integrations, it's unclear if customers are ready to trust critical infrastructure to a browser-based AI tool.

incumbent_threat.most_dangerous_competitor: Microsoft (Azure Energy, GridOS), Google (DeepMind Energy), OpenAI (via partnerships)
incumbent_threat.killer_feature: A major cloud provider launches an integrated, AI-powered grid modeling suite with direct hooks into utility data, regulatory compliance, and existing enterprise workflows—offered for free or bundled with cloud credits.
incumbent_threat.time_to_threat: 3-6 months if the market shows traction

hidden_assumptions: Utilities and grid operators are willing to trust and adopt browser-based AI tools for mission-critical planning.
There is a unified, accessible, and clean dataset for grid modeling (which is rarely true).
AI can outperform or match the accuracy and explainability of traditional engineering models in grid planning.
Regulators will approve AI-driven decisions for grid infrastructure.
Incumbents won't quickly replicate any unique features.

moat_reality.claimed_moat: Unified, trusted network model with AI-powered planning, built by ex-National Grid and Octopus Energy engineers.
moat_reality.actual_moat: No visible technical moat—no proprietary data, algorithms, or integrations shown. The trust and network effects they claim are not demonstrated, and the team pedigree alone is not defensible.

contrarian_analysis.honest_take: Squid talks a big game but shows almost nothing: no product, no code, no real traction—just Y Combinator branding and a waitlist. Unless they ship something real and prove utilities will actually use it, this is pure vaporware with a high risk of being crushed by better-resourced incumbents.

primary_trigger.trigger: Recent advances in AI-powered simulation and modeling frameworks enable real-time, scalable analysis of complex power grid networks.
primary_trigger.evidence: Emergence of open-source AI libraries and cloud-based simulation platforms in 2023-2024; increased adoption of ML for grid optimization in academic and industry publications.

secondary_triggers.trigger: Surge in grid modernization and renewable integration investments by utilities and governments.
secondary_triggers.relevance: Utilities need advanced planning tools to handle distributed energy resources and grid reliability, creating demand for modern solutions like Squid.

secondary_triggers.trigger: New mandates for grid resilience and transparency (e.g., US FERC Order 2222, EU Clean Energy Package).
secondary_triggers.relevance: Regulations require utilities to adopt better modeling and reporting tools, driving adoption of innovative platforms.

secondary_triggers.trigger: Incumbents (ETAP, DIgSILENT, Siemens) have slow product cycles and legacy interfaces.
secondary_triggers.relevance: Customers are frustrated by outdated tools and seeking more agile, AI-driven alternatives.

secondary_triggers.trigger: Engineering teams increasingly collaborate remotely and expect cloud-native, integrated workspaces.
secondary_triggers.relevance: Squid's collaborative platform matches new work patterns and expectations, unlike legacy desktop software.

why_now.why_not_earlier: AI simulation frameworks, cloud infrastructure, and regulatory pressure were not mature or widespread enough to support a scalable, collaborative grid modeling platform two years ago.
why_now.why_not_later: Delaying risks losing early market share to incumbents updating their offerings or new entrants leveraging the same AI breakthroughs.
why_now.newsletter_hook: AI breakthroughs and new grid regulations make now the perfect moment for Squid’s collaborative power network modeling platform.

takeaways.title: Move Heavy Workloads to the Browser
takeaways.insight: Running complex modeling and analysis directly in the browser (using WebAssembly, Web Workers, or similar) enables real-time collaboration and removes desktop install friction. This also allows for instant updates and easier onboarding.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Identify CPU-bound or interactive workloads that can be compiled to WebAssembly or run in browser threads. Use frameworks like Pyodide or AssemblyScript to port critical logic and expose APIs via the browser.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: If your workloads require GPUs, massive datasets, or have strict security/compliance requirements that browsers can't meet.

takeaways.title: Build a Unified, Versioned Data Model
takeaways.insight: A single source-of-truth data model (e.g., graph DB for grid topology) with built-in versioning and consensus mechanisms reduces fragmentation and enables real-time, multi-user collaboration.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Adopt a graph database (e.g., Neo4j, AWS Neptune) or CRDT-based data layer. Implement version control (Git-like or OT/CRDT) for model changes, and expose APIs for stakeholders to interact with the same data.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: If your domain doesn't require multi-user edits or if simple CRUD suffices.

takeaways.title: Leverage Edge Compute for Low-Latency Collaboration
takeaways.insight: Distributing compute and state sync closer to users (via edge functions or CDN workers) minimizes latency for real-time collaboration and model updates.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Deploy state synchronization or compute logic on edge platforms (e.g., Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Edge Functions). Use WebSockets or WebRTC for real-time updates.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: If your app is single-user or latency is not critical.
takeaways.example:
// Example: Edge function for model sync
export default async function(request) { /* sync logic */ }

takeaways.title: Modern, Intuitive UX for Complex Domains
takeaways.insight: A browser-based, modern UI dramatically lowers the barrier for domain experts used to legacy, clunky desktop tools. This accelerates adoption and reduces training/support costs.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Invest in UX research with target users. Use frameworks like React, Svelte, or Figma prototypes to iterate quickly. Prioritize real-time feedback and collaborative features.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: If your users are highly technical and prefer CLI or scripting interfaces.
takeaways.example:
// Example: Real-time collaborative UI component
<CollaborativeDiagram data={networkModel} />

takeaways.title: Minimize Server Spend by Offloading to Client
takeaways.insight: By pushing computation and state to the browser, you reduce server load and infra costs, especially for simulation-heavy workloads. This is a cost-effective alternative to scaling backend compute.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Audit your backend to identify tasks that can be safely executed on the client. Refactor to run these in-browser, and only sync essential results to the server.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: If client hardware is insufficient or you need centralized audit trails.

quick_wins: Prototype a WebAssembly or Pyodide-based browser module for a core algorithm.
Map your current data flows to identify fragmentation and potential for a unified model.
Test a real-time collaborative UI component using Yjs or similar CRDT library.
deeper_dives: Research CRDTs and OT for conflict-free real-time collaboration.
Evaluate edge compute platforms for stateful, low-latency workloads.
Study graph database options for representing complex network topologies.

headlines.headline: Why Pre Squid is betting AGAINST on-prem grid simulation (with just a browser tab)
headlines.subheadline: Squid is ditching decades of desktop-bound tradition, but with no product or traction, is the cloud-native gamble anything more than YC hype?

headlines.headline: I reverse-engineered Pre Squid’s pitch: No code, no product, just a waitlist and YC
headlines.subheadline: Behind the AI-powered grid planning claims, Squid’s public footprint is vapor—here’s what they’re really building (or not).

headlines.headline: Pre Squid is using the Figma playbook for grid simulation: All-in-browser, zero install
headlines.subheadline: Just like Figma upended design tools, Squid wants to move utilities off legacy software—if they can actually ship.

headlines.headline: The vertical data moat every grid AI startup should steal from Pre Squid
headlines.subheadline: Squid’s approach to capturing utility data could be a defensible edge—if they ever launch a real product.

headlines.headline: Is Pre Squid just vaporware riding the YC hype cycle? Let’s find out.
headlines.subheadline: With no traction, no code, and only a waitlist, is Squid’s browser-based grid planning anything more than a pitch deck?

best_headline.headline: Is Pre Squid just vaporware riding the YC hype cycle? Let’s find out.
best_headline.reason: This headline directly addresses the skepticism in the market, taps into the contrarian and spicy narrative, and promises a real investigation—perfect for an audience tired of empty startup claims.

viral_hooks.social_media_version: Is Pre Squid just vaporware riding the YC hype cycle? No code, no traction, just a waitlist—let’s dig in.

hook.opening_line: What if the future of our power grids depended on a browser tab?
hook.stakes: The reliability and modernization of electricity networks hinge on breaking free from outdated, fragmented planning tools.
hook.why_now: As AI and cloud-native tech reshape every industry, utilities face mounting pressure to upgrade aging infrastructure and workflows—before the next blackout.

discovery.surface_story: Pre Squid claims to offer an AI-powered workspace for grid planning, promising real-time collaboration and unified network modeling, all in your browser.
discovery.deeper_story: Behind the pitch: Squid is betting on cloud-native, serverless architectures and graph-based data models to replace legacy, desktop-bound grid simulation—an ambitious technical leap in a risk-averse industry.
discovery.surprise: Despite bold claims and YC branding, there’s no public product, code, or traction—raising questions about execution and credibility.

analysis.technical_insight: Squid’s approach to a unified, real-time grid model means tackling versioning, consensus, and collaborative editing—likely with CRDTs and WebAssembly modules, which are rare in this domain.
analysis.competitive_insight: If successful, Squid could build a vertical data moat, locking in grid operators with a seamless, AI-augmented workflow. But incumbents with deeper pockets and existing relationships loom large.
analysis.tension: Bull case: Squid delivers a breakthrough, wins early adopters, and sets a new standard. Bear case: It’s vaporware, outpaced by established players or stymied by utility inertia.

resolution.takeaway: Building a browser-native, collaborative grid planning tool is a non-trivial technical and market challenge—watch for real product and traction before buying the hype.
resolution.prediction: Squid will need to ship a working demo and prove utility engagement within the next 12 months or risk fading into obscurity.
resolution.call_to_action: Builders: Prototype collaborative modules and map data fragmentation now; readers: track Squid’s public releases and utility partnerships for real signals.

suggested_visuals: Diagram of legacy grid planning workflow vs Squid’s proposed unified model
Browser screenshot mockup of collaborative grid editing
Competitive landscape chart: Squid vs incumbents

unique_voice_content:
```markdown
## Pre Squid: AI for Power Grids—Or Just PowerPoint?

"AI-powered grid planning in your browser" sounds like catnip for anyone sick of fighting with Excel macros and crusty on-prem simulators. But before you dust off your resume for the energy transition, let's ask: is Pre Squid shipping code, or just shipping a vision deck?

### Core Insight

Here's the nugget: Pre Squid wants to do for power grid modeling what Figma did for design—real-time, collaborative, cloud-native, and (allegedly) AI-augmented. They’re betting that utilities will trade their Frankenstein workflows (spreadsheets, ancient desktop apps, endless PDFs) for a unified, trusted network model that lives entirely in the browser. If you’ve ever untangled a legacy data pipeline, you know this is a gnarly, high-friction domain—ripe for a vertical SaaS with real technical depth. The interesting bit is less "AI magic" and more about orchestrating messy, fragmented enterprise data in a way grid planners might actually trust.

### Technical Meat

On paper, Squid is chasing a hard technical prize: live, collaborative grid modeling, with heavy-duty simulation and analysis, all in the browser. That’s a big leap from the desktop-bound, Fortran-hobbled world of grid ops. If they’re really pushing serverless or edge compute for this, hats off—that’s non-trivial engineering, especially if latency and security are non-negotiable (spoiler: they are, if you’re touching anything critical infrastructure).

But here’s the rub: there’s zero public evidence of how they’re pulling this off. No open-source repos, no demos, no screenshots. Their "AI" story is a black box (unless you count the phrase "AI-powered" on the landing page), and there’s no indication of actual LLM integration, custom models, or even a whiff of differentiated data. Their stack is "AWS, probably, but who knows?" The wildest thing about Squid right now is how little technical detail exists. For all we know, the "grid" here is a Figma file with some fake wires.

Where they get credit: the founding team has legit energy sector scars (ex-National Grid, Octopus, AWS), so they probably know the pain points. Targeting browser-first, collaborative workflows in this domain is a smart wedge if—and it’s a big if—they can actually ship. But with no product, no code, and only a waitlist to show for it, this mostly feels like a YC-backed pitch to attract FOMO investors, not grid engineers.

### Honest Assessment

Would I use this? Not unless they show something real. If you’re a utility CTO, you’re not swapping your risk-averse, auditor-pleasing workflow for a stealth SaaS with zero public track record. If you’re building in energy tech, Squid’s thesis is worth watching, but as of today, it’s vaporware. If you want to see real AI for critical infrastructure, go find a team that ships code, not just hype.

### Builder Takeaway

- **Vertical SaaS is hard mode:** Deep domain + real-time collaboration + AI = serious engineering; hand-waving won’t cut it.
- **Show, don’t tell:** If you’re claiming an AI moat, prove it—ship a demo, publish a paper, open-source something.
- **Team pedigree buys attention, not adoption:** Ex-bigco founders help with intros but don’t replace technical proof.
- **Transparency matters:** Even in stealth, leave breadcrumbs for builders and skeptics to follow—closed doors kill curiosity.
- **Don’t over-index on “AI-powered”:** Real differentiation comes from data, integrations, and workflow impact, not buzzwords.

### Prediction

In two years, Squid either ships a real, trusted browser-based planner and lands a lighthouse utility—or gets out-executed by incumbents with deeper pockets and actual traction. Happy to change my tune if they drop a public demo and show actual AI under the hood. Until then, call me skeptical.
```
